trajectory_summary: The call was structurally unworkable from the moment Carlin disclosed his self-install intent at 06:13 — Ion's model requires professional installation, and no setter move recovers that. The more instructive shape is the 90 seconds before that disclosure: Carlin was actively engaged, sharing real electrical loads (chicken heaters, greenhouse 220V), and asking for pricing. Ethan had bill-anchor and cross-sell material in front of him and responded with 'Uh-huh. Okay. Yeah. That's cool.' The call leaked energy not because of the disqualifier but because the rep was passive during the window when the customer was most open.
outcome_dispute: The v1 outcome tag is listed as 'unknown.' The correct classification is disqualified — Carlin intends to self-install, which Ion cannot accommodate. There is no appointment, no tentative booking, and no locked callback time. The rep's 'I'll try calling some other time' at 06:43 is a soft exit with no commitment anchor, not a deferred appointment.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Bill-anchor passivity during peak customer engagement
primary_coaching_focus.why: Between 04:44 and 05:53, Carlin described active electrical loads (chicken heaters, greenhouse 220V), multiple project ideas, and genuine enthusiasm for solar. This was the call's highest-energy window — and Ethan responded with filler acknowledgments on every beat. The codex's bill_anchoring pattern is 'ask + acknowledge + flip'; Ethan did none of the three across the entire window. This is the most coachable gap on this call because it's not about a hard disqualifier — it's about what Ethan did with the customer's own energy when it was highest.

key_moments.quote: I'm not gonna put it on my roof.
key_moments.stewart_read: Ethan spent 15 seconds narrating a Google Maps roof assessment before asking a single question about installation preferences. Carlin's correction is the direct consequence. Strategic read: Ethan was buying time while pulling up the address. Reactive read: the narration was filler that telegraphed a script assumption the customer hadn't confirmed. The reactive read holds — the narration created a false premise Carlin had to correct, costing rapport and redirecting the call.

key_moments.quote: I need to know how much it's gonna cost, then I'll know what direction to go.
key_moments.stewart_read: This is the codex's price_estimate_before_design trigger verbatim. The canonical rebuttal exists: redirect to 'let me get you an actual design with real numbers — not just a random ballpark.' Ethan instead deferred to a future callback ('we'd have to wait until that's completed'), which is procedurally accurate but misses the curiosity-creation opportunity entirely. The rebuttal's job here was to keep Carlin engaged long enough to surface the bill and the electrical loads — which Carlin then volunteered anyway at 05:15 and 05:37.

key_moments.quote: I've got heaters on them right now, it takes me to get down right about 10 degrees before the things they all shivering all over.
key_moments.stewart_read: Carlin is describing active, ongoing electrical loads — chicken-coop heaters running through a Virginia winter. This is the most concrete bill-anchor opportunity in the call. The customer is not talking abstractly about solar; he is describing real current consumption that solar could offset. Ethan's response ('That's good.') is a filler acknowledgment that drops the anchor entirely. The codex's capture pattern is 'ask + acknowledge + flip' — Ethan did none of the three.

key_moments.quote: I also got, two twenty in my greenhouse running that so that my plants won't freeze.
key_moments.stewart_read: A 220V greenhouse is a discrete secondary structure with its own electrical load — a direct match for the codex's TBD item 'solar_for_secondary_structure.' This is separate from the chicken-run discussion: Carlin has now described two non-roof structures with active electrical loads. Ethan's response is silence followed by 'Okay. Well, yeah, and that's—' before Carlin cuts back in. No bridge to Ion's capabilities, no curiosity question about the greenhouse's energy cost.
